result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a story begins, that invisible hinge that swings open the world we think we know. It’s the breath that’s half in your lungs and half still outside in the room, the pause between the ring of an alarm and the decision to open your eyes. Most nights I sit at my desk, lights off except for the small ceramic lamp that looks like a Japanese moon someone cracked open and glued back together with gold. The cracks glow warmer than the porcelain around them, which feels like a secret philosophy: the damage is what conducts the light. I like to write longhand in the dimness, letting the pen surprise me so that the page looks afterward like a map of earthquakes. The ink climbs and dives and sometimes digs a trench right through the paper, and I imagine future archaeologists mistaking those ruptures for the fossilized flight paths of invisible birds.

Tonight the window is open to the first true scent of May, a complicated perfume of lilac, asphalt cooling from the day, and whatever mysterious mineral the river exhales after dark. Somewhere down the block a neighbor is learning to play the saxophone, repeating the same eight bruised notes until they start to sound like forgiveness. I picture the brass catching the lamplight inside his apartment, a small sun he can carry from room to room, domesticating gravity. We never speak, but sound is a communal bloodstream. When he finally masters the phrase, I feel it in my wrist, the way a parent feels the school-bus pulling away.

In the margins of my notebook I draw cages without doors, bicycles with square wheels, a city skyline made entirely of question marks. The drawings are excuses to keep the pen moving while some underground part of me digs toward whatever wants to be said. I used to believe I needed a spectacular subject—eruptions, revolutions, the kind of love that ends with one person lighting the other’s name on fire above the Arctic Circle. Now I’m more interested in the microscopic: the hush that lives inside the letter h, the pause a carrot takes before deciding to grow left or right when it meets a stone. I want to write the biography of dust, the autobiography of the color blue. I want to write the moment you realize your parents have become abstract paintings—still vivid, still recognizable, but shrinking every year as they recede toward a horizon you can’t yet visit.

Memory, I’ve noticed, behaves like a flock of European starlings. Thousands of identical facts wheel overhead, suddenly pivoting in perfect synchronicity, forming a silhouette that looks exactly like the outline of your old bedroom. Then, in the next instant, they disperse and reassemble into the face of the dog you lost when you were seven. Nothing ever vanishes; it only changes shape, density, direction. The brain is a dark suitcase forgotten on an attic floor; shake it and every winter coat releases the cough of its previous owner. I carry my grandfather’s cough the way others carry a gene for blue eyes. Sometimes it escapes me during meetings, and coworkers politely pretend I merely cleared my throat.

People ask where writers get ideas, expecting a geography: a hidden mountain, a password-protected website, a flea market that sells plots by the kilo. The truth is more unspectacular: ideas are the lint that collects in the pockets of daily boredom. You forget a towel in the washing machine; forty-eight hours later the drum smells like a cave. That sourness whispers, What if nostalgia had a flavor? And suddenly you’re writing about a woman who bottles the scent of abandoned laundry and sells it to homesick astronauts. The story is stupid until, paragraph six, you realize you’re describing your mother’s depression when the last child left for college. The towel reappears as a white flag the woman waves on the moon’s dark side. Fiction is just the alphabet’s way of apologizing for the truth’s sting.

I worry, sometimes, that we are eroding the quality of silence. Not silence as absence but silence as architecture: the vaulted quiet that used to live between lightning and thunder, the hush that once hovered above landlines after the caller hung up but before the dial tone remembered to speak. Now every pause is filled with a playlist, a podcast, the soft click of algorithmic thumbs. We scroll past someone else’s sunrise while the one outside our own kitchen window drains of color, embarrassed to be seen. I want to start a rental service that offers silence by the hour: curated, wireless-free quiet delivered in mason jars like midnight cut from the mountains before the stars noticed. Customers would sign waivers acknowledging that prolonged exposure might cause spontaneous recall of childhood prayers, or the urge to write letters in actual cursive. Profit margins would be slim; the product expires the instant you open the lid and someone coughs three apartments away.

The cat jumps onto the desk, treading across the page with the imperious indifference of a medieval pope. She leaves tiny gray ellipses that dry into accidental Braille. If I could read them they would probably say, Feed me, or perhaps, The moon you keep on your desk is a counterfeit, I should know, I interview the real one through the window every night. She settles across my forearm, pinning the pen. Fur drifts onto the wet ink like dandelion seeds trying to colonize a planet. I decide the interruption is part of the story, the way detours sometimes become the pilgrimage. Every sentence is a collaboration with gravity, inconvenience, the neighbor who chooses this exact moment to drag his trash cans past my open window, creating a slow comet of sound that drags its tin tail across the sky of the paragraph. I could edit him out later, but why pretend I write in a vacuum? The vacuum is the one place even silence cannot breathe.

Under the lamp’s gold fissures I notice my hands look older than I feel. Tendons glide like cautious swans beneath thinning skin. If hands are maps, mine have reached the region labeled Here Be Strange Fruit. They remember vinyl records, rotary phones, the weight of a card catalog drawer pulled out almost far enough to tip. They will someday, inevitably, forget how to hold a pen, but by then maybe language will have migrated to some other appendage. I imagine future poets composing with eyebrow gestures, entire epics performed in Morse-code blinks. Reviews will complain that contemporary literature lacks ankle.

Outside, the saxophone finally surrenders to the night’s deeper jazz. The last note climbs out the window, finds the lilac, and settles there, a purple vibration. In its ascent I hear the echo of every story I haven’t written, circling like moths that mistake the moon for a way home. I close the notebook, not because I’m finished but because the page is full of doors, and someone has to decide when to knock. The lamp keeps humming its cracked lullaby. The cat dreams of tributaries made of tuna. The river continues its slow grammar lesson: sentence, evaporate, rain, repeat. Somewhere between the pen and the dark, the thousandth word arrives exactly on time, wearing the soft shoes of a stranger who has walked these rooms forever, waiting for me to notice the threshold.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
